summary: Joel announces the creation of new sub-forums for the Joel on Software discussion group, which had grown too large for productive conversation. He draws a parallel to Usenet's historical tendency to fork newsgroups as they grew, noting his general aversion to forking because it dilutes discussion quality. Despite this, he concedes the practical necessity of splitting into topic-focused groups. He also briefly promotes the second printing of his book.
key_points: Online discussion communities naturally tend to fragment as they grow, following the pattern established by Usenet newsgroups
Forking discussions carries a real cost: smaller groups produce less interesting conversations
Practical necessity sometimes overrides philosophical preferences — too much traffic degrades discussion quality just as much as too little
Joel applies natural selection to his forums: groups that don't generate enough traffic will be killed off
money_quotes: I didn't like forks, because they make discussions less interesting.
It's bad enough there's a comp.software.windows.nt.40.microsoft.notepad, does there have to be a comp.software.windows.nt.40.microsoft.notepad.helpfile.index?
If any of these groups doesn't have enough daily traffic, it'll go the way of the old New Yorkers group and the Delphi group, bless them, in the Joel on Software graveyard.
key_insight: Growing online communities face an inherent tension between keeping discussions consolidated enough to be interesting and splitting them up enough to remain manageable.
